sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic procedural analgesia supports painful prehospital care such as splinting, reduction, packaging, and wound treatment. The aim is to reduce distress enough to perform necessary care safely without losing sight of airway, breathing, haemodynamics, or transport priority.

sections.title: Analgesic Options
sections.content:
- Choose the analgesic approach according to pain severity, urgency, monitoring capability, and the planned procedure
- Methoxyflurane can be useful when the patient can self-administer and the procedure is short
- Opioids may be effective but require respiratory vigilance
- Ketamine can be useful when strong analgesia is needed and maintaining haemodynamic stability matters
- Combining modalities may reduce the dose of any one agent, but it also increases the need for reassessment

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Procedural analgesia should be planned: know what you are trying to achieve and what monitoring or rescue you will need
- Reassess pain, conscious state, ventilation, and haemodynamics before, during, and after the procedure
- Good analgesia should facilitate the procedure, not create avoidable delay or oversedation
- Document the agent, dose, route, response, and any adverse effects
